From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Flowering plant in the heather family

| *Calluna* | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Flowering *Calluna vulgaris* | |
| [Scientific classification](/wiki/Taxonomy_(biology) "Taxonomy (biology)") [Edit this classification](/wiki/Template:Taxonomy/Calluna "Edit this classification") | |
| Kingdom: | [Plantae](/wiki/Plant "Plant") |
| *Clade*: | [Tracheophytes](/wiki/Vascular_plant "Vascular plant") |
| *Clade*: | [Angiosperms](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") |
| *Clade*: | [Eudicots](/wiki/Eudicots "Eudicots") |
| *Clade*: | [Asterids](/wiki/Asterids "Asterids") |
| Order: | [Ericales](/wiki/Ericales "Ericales") |
| Family: | [Ericaceae](/wiki/Ericaceae "Ericaceae") |
| Subfamily: | [Ericoideae](/wiki/Ericoideae "Ericoideae") |
| Tribe: | [Ericeae](/wiki/Ericeae "Ericeae") |
| Genus: | *Calluna* [Salisb.](/wiki/Richard_Anthony_Salisbury "Richard Anthony Salisbury") |
| Species: | ***C. vulgaris*** |
| [Binomial name](/wiki/Binomial_nomenclature "Binomial nomenclature") | |
| ***Calluna vulgaris***  ([L.](/wiki/Carl_Linnaeus "Carl Linnaeus")) Hull | |

***Calluna vulgaris***, **common heather**, **ling**, or simply **heather**,[[1]](#cite_note-1) is the sole species in the [genus](/wiki/Genus "Genus") ***Calluna*** in the [flowering plant](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") family [Ericaceae](/wiki/Ericaceae "Ericaceae"). It is a low-growing [evergreen](/wiki/Evergreen "Evergreen") [shrub](/wiki/Shrub "Shrub") growing to 20 to 50 centimetres (8 to 20 in) tall, or rarely to 1 metre (40 in) and taller,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and is found widely in Europe and Asia Minor on [acidic](/wiki/Acid "Acid") soils in open sunny situations and in moderate shade.

It is the dominant plant in most [heathland](/wiki/Heath "Heath") and [moorland](/wiki/Moorland "Moorland") in Europe, and in some [bog](/wiki/Bog "Bog") vegetation and acidic [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine") and [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") woodland. It is tolerant of grazing and regenerates following occasional burning, and is often managed in nature reserves and [grouse moors](/wiki/Grouse_moor "Grouse moor") by sheep or cattle grazing, and also by light burning.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calluna&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

*Calluna* can reach 60 centimetres (24 in) in height.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It has small-scale [leaves](/wiki/Leaves "Leaves") (less than 2–3 mm long) borne in opposite and [decussate](/wiki/Decussate "Decussate") pairs, whereas those of *[Erica](/wiki/Erica_(plant) "Erica (plant)")* are generally larger and in whorls of 3–4, sometimes 5.[[4]](#cite_note-Stace-4) It flowers from July to September.[[5]](#cite_note-CTW-5): 231 In wild plants these are normally [mauve](/wiki/Mauve "Mauve"), but white-flowered plants also occur occasionally. They are terminal in [racemes](/wiki/Raceme "Raceme") with sepal-like [bracts](/wiki/Bract "Bract") at the base with a superior ovary, the fruit a [capsule](/wiki/Capsule_(fruit) "Capsule (fruit)").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Unlike *Erica*, *Calluna* sometimes sports [double flowers](/wiki/Double-flowered "Double-flowered"). *Calluna* is sometimes referred to as Summer (or Autumn) heather to distinguish it from winter or spring flowering species of *Erica*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Chemistry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calluna&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Chemistry")]

[Phenolic compounds](/wiki/Phenolic_compound "Phenolic compound") in the shoots of *Calluna vulgaris* include [chlorogenic acid](/wiki/Chlorogenic_acid "Chlorogenic acid") and a novel phenolic glycoside, most of which are found in greater number during the summer.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The [nectar](/wiki/Nectar "Nectar") of *Calluna vulgaris* contains a megastigmane, callunene, that is inhibitory at naturally occurring concentrations to a common [trypanosome](/wiki/Trypanosomatida "Trypanosomatida") parasite of bumble bees, *[Crithidia bombi](/wiki/Crithidia_bombi "Crithidia bombi")*. Koch et al. elucidate the mechanism of activity that results in the loss of the parasite's flagellum, leading to reduced infectivity, because the flagellum is crucial to anchoring in the insect gut.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Taxonomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calluna&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Taxonomy")]

*Calluna* was separated from the closely related genus *[Erica](/wiki/Erica_(plant) "Erica (plant)")* by [Richard Anthony Salisbury](/wiki/Richard_Anthony_Salisbury "Richard Anthony Salisbury"), who devised the generic name *Calluna* probably from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language") *kallýnō* ([καλλύνω]) 'beautify, sweep clean', in reference to its traditional use in [besoms](/wiki/Besom "Besom"). The specific [epithet](/wiki/Binomial_nomenclature "Binomial nomenclature") *vulgaris* is [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") for 'common'. *Calluna* is differentiated from *Erica* by its [corolla](/wiki/Corolla_(flower) "Corolla (flower)") and [calyx](/wiki/Calyx_(botany) "Calyx (botany)") each being in four parts instead of five.

## Distribution and habitat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calluna&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Distribution and habitat")]

*Calluna vulgaris* is native to [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands"), and the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores").[[9]](#cite_note-powo-9) It has been introduced into many other places worldwide with suitable climates, including [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") and the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands").[[10]](#cite_note-issg-10) It is extremely cold-[hardy](/wiki/Hardiness_(plants) "Hardiness (plants)"), surviving severe exposure and freezing conditions well below −20 °C (−4 °F).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calluna&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ecology")]

Heather is an important food source for various sheep and deer which can graze the tips of the plants when snow covers low-growing vegetation. [Willow grouse](/wiki/Willow_grouse "Willow grouse") and [red grouse](/wiki/Red_grouse "Red grouse") feed on the young shoots and seeds of this plant.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Both adult and [larva](/wiki/Larva "Larva") of the heather beetle (*[Lochmaea suturalis](/wiki/Lochmaea_suturalis "Lochmaea suturalis")*) feed on it, and can cause extensive mortality in some instances. The larvae of a number of [Lepidoptera](/wiki/Lepidoptera "Lepidoptera") species also feed on the plant, notably the small emperor moth *[Saturnia pavonia](/wiki/Saturnia_pavonia "Saturnia pavonia")*.

### As an invasive species

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calluna&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: As an invasive species")]

The plant was introduced to New Zealand and has become an [invasive weed](/wiki/Invasive_species "Invasive species") in some areas, notably the [Tongariro National Park](/wiki/Tongariro_National_Park "Tongariro National Park") and [Mount Ruapehu](/wiki/Mount_Ruapehu "Mount Ruapehu") in the North Island, as well as the Wilderness Reserve (Te Anau) in the South Island, overgrowing native plants. [Heather beetles](/wiki/Lochmaea_suturalis "Lochmaea suturalis") have been released to stop the heather, with preliminary trials successful to date.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Cultivars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calluna&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cultivars")]

There are many named [cultivars](/wiki/Cultivar "Cultivar"), selected for variation in flower colour and for different foliage colour and growing habits.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Different cultivars have flower colours ranging from white, through pink and a wide range of purples, and including reds. The flowering season with different cultivars extends from late July to November in the northern hemisphere. The flowers may turn brown but still remain on the plants over winter, and this can lead to interesting decorative effects. Cultivars with ornamental foliage are usually selected for reddish and golden leaf colour. A few forms can be silvery grey. Many of the ornamental foliage forms change colour with the onset of winter weather, usually increasing in intensity of colour. Some forms are grown for distinctive young spring foliage.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The following cultivars have gained the [Royal Horticultural Society](/wiki/Royal_Horticultural_Society "Royal Horticultural Society")'s [Award of Garden Merit](/wiki/Award_of_Garden_Merit "Award of Garden Merit"):

* 'Alicia' (Garden Girls series)[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* 'Annemarie' [[18]](#cite_note-18)
* 'Beoley Gold'[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* 'County Wicklow'[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* 'Dark Beauty'[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* 'Dark Star'[[22]](#cite_note-22)
* 'Darkness' [[23]](#cite_note-23)
* 'Elsie Purnell'[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* 'Firefly'[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* 'Kerstin' [[26]](#cite_note-26)
* 'Kinlochruel' [[27]](#cite_note-27)
* 'Peter Sparkes'[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* 'Robert Chapman' [[29]](#cite_note-29)
* 'Silver Queen'[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* 'Sister Anne'[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* 'Spring Cream'[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* 'Tib' [[33]](#cite_note-33)
* 'Velvet Fascination'[[34]](#cite_note-34)
* 'Wickwar Flame'[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* 'White Coral'[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calluna&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Uses")]

Formerly heather was used to dye wool yellow and to tan leather. With [malt](/wiki/Malt "Malt"), heather is an ingredient in [gruit](/wiki/Gruit "Gruit"), a mixture of flavourings used in the brewing of [heather-beer](/wiki/Beer_in_Scotland "Beer in Scotland") during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") before the use of [hops](/wiki/Hops "Hops"). [Thomas Pennant](/wiki/Thomas_Pennant "Thomas Pennant") wrote in *A Tour in Scotland* (1769) that on the Scottish island of [Islay](/wiki/Islay "Islay") "ale is frequently made of the young tops of heath, mixing two thirds of that plant with one of malt, sometimes adding hops".[[37]](#cite_note-37) "Heath Beer" is mentioned in the recipe book of [Lady Ann Fanshawe](/wiki/Ann,_Lady_Fanshawe "Ann, Lady Fanshawe") (compiled from 1651).[[38]](#cite_note-38)

From time immemorial heather has been used for making [besoms](/wiki/Besom "Besom"), a practice recorded in "[Buy Broom Buzzems](/wiki/Broom_Buzzems "Broom Buzzems")" a song probably written by [William Purvis (Blind Willie)](/wiki/William_Purvis_(Blind_Willie) "William Purvis (Blind Willie)") (1752–1832) from [Newcastle-upon-Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle-upon-Tyne "Newcastle-upon-Tyne"), England.

Heather [honey](/wiki/Honey "Honey") is a highly valued product in moorland and heathland areas, with many [beehives](/wiki/Beehives "Beehives") being moved there in late summer. Not always as valued as it is today,[[39]](#cite_note-39) it was dismissed as *mel improbum*, "unwholesome honey" by [Dioscurides](/wiki/Dioscurides "Dioscurides").[[40]](#cite_note-40) Heather honey has a characteristic strong taste, and an unusual texture, for it is [thixotropic](/wiki/Thixotropy "Thixotropy"), being a [jelly](/wiki/Gel "Gel") until stirred, when it becomes a syrup like other honey, but then sets again to a jelly. This makes the [extraction](/wiki/Honey_extraction "Honey extraction") of the honey from the comb difficult, and it is therefore often sold as [comb honey](/wiki/Comb_honey "Comb honey").

White heather is regarded in Scotland as being lucky,[[41]](#cite_note-41) a tradition brought from [Balmoral](/wiki/Balmoral_Castle "Balmoral Castle") to England by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria")[[42]](#cite_note-42) and sprigs of it are often sold as a [charm](/wiki/Good_luck_charm "Good luck charm") and worked into bridal [bouquets](/wiki/Flower_bouquet "Flower bouquet").

Heather stalks are used by a small industry in Scotland as a raw material for sentimental jewellery. The stalks are stripped of bark, dyed in bright colours and then compressed with resin.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

*Calluna vulgaris* herb has been used in the traditional Austrian medicine internally as tea for treatment of disorders of the kidneys and urinary tract.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## In culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Calluna&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In culture")]

Heather is seen as iconic of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), where the plant grows widely. When poems like *Bonnie Auld Scotland* speak of "fragrant hills of purple heather', when the hero of [*Kidnapped*](/wiki/Kidnapped_(novel) "Kidnapped (novel)") flees through the heather, when heather and Scotland are linked in the same sentence, the heather talked about is *Calluna vulgaris*.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Purple heather is one of the two [national flowers](/wiki/Floral_emblem "Floral emblem") of [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"),[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-norwayguide-2022-46) the other being *[Saxifraga cotyledon](/wiki/Saxifraga_cotyledon "Saxifraga cotyledon")*. It was chosen as a national flower on the basis of a vote of popularity in a Norwegian radio show in 1976.[[46]](#cite_note-norwayguide-2022-46)

*Calluna vulgaris* is the [province flower](/wiki/Province_flowers_of_Sweden "Province flowers of Sweden") of the Swedish province of [Västergötland](/wiki/V%C3%A4sterg%C3%B6tland "Västergötland").
